logistics.drive_notes: I-70 westbound. Traction laws frequently active. Severe weekend traffic bottlenecks at the Eisenhower Tunnel.
logistics.lodging_notes: Silverthorne, Frisco, and Dillon serve as central budget hubs. On-mountain lodging is premium.

metadata.reputation: Denver's local mountain. Famous for endless moguls and great tree skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible glades (Mary Jane), heavily bumped out main runs, steep alpine chutes in the Cirque.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Consistently good cold snow. Winds can scour the top but fill in the trees nicely.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very high on weekends, but the Mary Jane side spreads experts out well.
metadata.logistics_notes: The Winter Park Express train runs directly from Denver Union Station right to the lifts.

metadata.reputation: High-alpine soul mountain with the longest season in Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Legendary steep hike-to chutes (East Wall) and intense high-alpine bowls. Very little beginner terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely high base elevation guarantees cold, dry snow when lower resorts get heavy snow or rain.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low mid-week, moderate on weekends. Highly favored by locals.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires driving over Loveland Pass (US-6) which frequently closes during storms.

metadata.reputation: No-frills, old-school local favorite situated directly on the Continental Divide.
metadata.terrain_character: Wide open, above-treeline bowls. Lift 9 accesses incredible high-alpine ridges.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches massive wind-buff. Stays incredibly cold.
metadata.crowd_factor: Generally low. Avoids the worst I-70 crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Located *before* the Eisenhower tunnel, saving 30+ minutes of traffic compared to Summit County.

metadata.reputation: Perfectly naturally-separated terrain from beginner to expert.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible high alpine bowls (Tucker Mountain) and great sustained pitch.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Classic Colorado dry powder. High elevation keeps it well preserved.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on weekends, but the natural separation prevents extreme bottlenecks.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free parking available in Alpine lot with shuttles.

metadata.reputation: Massive resort attached to a historic mining town. Incredible high alpine.
metadata.terrain_character: High alpine bowls (Imperial Express) are amazing, but the lower mountain acts as a flat bottleneck.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High elevation holds snow perfectly, but high winds above treeline can severely scour the bowls.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely crowded. The lower mountain base areas get heavily congested.
metadata.logistics_notes: Parking in town is expensive; relies heavily on gondola access from the main lots.

metadata.reputation: Family-heavy frontside with a massive, rugged footprint in the back.
metadata.terrain_character: Dercum Mountain is all cruisers, but The Outback and North Peak offer incredibly steep, tight tree skiing.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: The Outback holds powder for days because the frontside crowds rarely venture back that far.
metadata.crowd_factor: Frontside is a zoo; the Outback hides the crowds perfectly.
metadata.logistics_notes: Offers some of the best night skiing in Colorado.

metadata.reputation: Sprawling luxury mega-resort with world-famous Back Bowls.
metadata.terrain_character: Endless open bowl skiing, deep glades in Blue Sky Basin, and massive front-side groomers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Great snow, but the sheer volume of skiers tracks out the main runs quickly.
metadata.crowd_factor: Front-side bottlenecks are brutal, but advanced riders can easily escape crowds in the back bowls.
metadata.logistics_notes: Extremely expensive parking. Best to carpool or use the bus from outer lots.

metadata.reputation: Luxury grooming and free cookies, often overlooked by powder chasers.
metadata.terrain_character: Impeccable groomers, but Grouse Mountain hides incredibly steep, demanding tree runs and bump lines.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Slightly lower elevation means the base can get warm late season, but the upper trees hold snow well.
metadata.crowd_factor: Consistently less crowded than Vail or Breckenridge on a powder day.
metadata.logistics_notes: Further down I-70, adding 20+ mins to the drive from Denver.

logistics.drive_notes: Long drive past Glenwood Canyon. Highway 82 can be treacherous.
logistics.lodging_notes: Aspen/Snowmass proper is luxury pricing. Budget lodging requires a 40+ minute commute from down-valley (Basalt, Carbondale, or Glenwood Springs).

metadata.reputation: The sprawling giant of the Aspen area. Something for everyone.
metadata.terrain_character: Massive vertical, incredible hike-to alpine terrain (Hanging Valley), and endless cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Consistently excellent cold, dry powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Moderate. Its massive size absorbs crowds effortlessly.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free shuttle buses connect all four Aspen mountains.

metadata.reputation: Classic steep skiing dumping right into the heart of downtown Aspen.
metadata.terrain_character: No green runs. Steep sustained pitches, tight trees, and heavy bumps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Holds snow well on the upper mountain, but the face can get baked in the afternoon.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low to moderate on powder days. Most people favor Snowmass for sheer acreage.
metadata.logistics_notes: You can walk to the gondola from downtown Aspen.

metadata.reputation: The crown jewel of Colorado steep skiing.
metadata.terrain_character: Highlands Bowl is a legendary 45-minute hike-to powder experience. Insanely steep.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Highlands Bowl preserves powder for days after a storm due to its aspect.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. The physical effort required keeps the crowds away.
metadata.logistics_notes: Not a mountain for beginners or casual cruising.

metadata.reputation: Home of the X-Games, but primarily known as a beginner mountain.
metadata.terrain_character: Very flat, rolling groomers and massive terrain parks. Tiehack side has some decent pitched cruisers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Lower elevation means snow quality degrades faster than Highlands or Snowmass.
metadata.crowd_factor: Virtually empty on powder days since all the experts go to Highlands or Aspen Mountain.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great option if you want untouched corduroy or massive terrain parks.

metadata.reputation: A hidden, low-cost local gem just outside Glenwood Springs.
metadata.terrain_character: One of the steepest sustained pitches in the state (The Heathen). Great, tight tree runs.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: A bit lower in elevation, but heavily protected by trees.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Mostly locals from Glenwood Springs.
metadata.logistics_notes: Offers a much cheaper alternative to the Aspen mountains while still catching similar storm tracks.

logistics.drive_notes: Rabbit Ears Pass can be highly dangerous during a storm. Bypasses the worst of I-70.
logistics.lodging_notes: Plenty of lodging in Steamboat Springs, ranging from budget to luxury.

metadata.reputation: The 'Champagne Powder' capital of Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Arguably the best perfectly-spaced tree skiing in the state (Shadows and Closets). Very few steep chutes.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unique northern storm cells that miss I-70 completely. Snow is incredibly light.
metadata.crowd_factor: High on powder days, but massive acreage spreads it out.
metadata.logistics_notes: Base elevation is very low for Colorado (6,900ft), making the very bottom susceptible to warming.

logistics.drive_notes: Extreme high-friction drive from Denver. Requires a multi-day trip commitment.
logistics.lodging_notes: Telluride is wildly expensive. Purgatory/Durango offers much more affordable options.

metadata.reputation: The most beautiful mountain town in America with jaw-dropping extreme terrain.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible steep chutes, massive hike-to terrain (Palmyra Peak), and long, gorgeous groomers.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches southern storms. High elevation preserves the powder perfectly.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Its extreme isolation prevents casual weekend crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Free gondola connects the town to the mountain village.

metadata.reputation: Laid back, family-friendly resort near Durango.
metadata.terrain_character: Cruisy, undulating terrain. Great, widely-spaced trees. Lacks extreme steeps.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Needs a good southern storm track, but holds snow well.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Walk-on lifts are common even on powder days.
metadata.logistics_notes: 30-minute drive north of Durango.

metadata.reputation: Hardcore, experts-only freeride mecca.
metadata.terrain_character: Un-groomed, intense avalanche terrain. Only one lift; heavily reliant on hiking or heli-drops.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Extremely high elevation. Deep, untouched powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Capped daily capacity. Zero crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Requires avalanche gear (beacon, shovel, probe). Guided-only for most of the primary powder season.

logistics.drive_notes: Wolf Creek Pass is notoriously treacherous during snowstorms.
logistics.lodging_notes: No on-mountain lodging. Stay in Pagosa Springs (30 mins west) or South Fork (30 mins east).

metadata.reputation: The undisputed snow magnet of Colorado.
metadata.terrain_character: Consistently gets the most snow in the state. Short but fun, steep pitches and hike-to ridges.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: High base elevation guarantees deep, cold powder.
metadata.crowd_factor: Very low. Remote location keeps lift lines to a minimum.
metadata.logistics_notes: Incredibly affordable lift tickets compared to mega-resorts.

logistics.drive_notes: Route 50 over Monarch pass can be difficult, but typically avoids I-70 gridlock.
logistics.lodging_notes: Gunnison and Salida offer cheap lodging. Mt. Crested Butte is more premium.

metadata.reputation: Extreme, rocky steeps and a fiercely independent town culture.
metadata.terrain_character: The Extremes (North Face, Headwall) are some of the steepest lift-accessed terrain in North America.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredibly rocky. Needs a massive base to safely open the best terrain.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low. The drive distance keeps casual Denver weekenders away.
metadata.logistics_notes: About a 4 to 4.5 hour drive from Denver depending on weather.

metadata.reputation: 100% all-natural snow. A true local's gem straddling the Continental Divide.
metadata.terrain_character: Mirkwood basin offers incredible hike-to powder. Very fun, rolling natural terrain.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Incredible snow preservation due to high base elevation and zero snowmaking.
metadata.crowd_factor: Low midweek, but tiny base area creates severe bottlenecks on powder weekends when locals flock there.
metadata.logistics_notes: About a 3 to 3.5 hour drive from Denver.

logistics.drive_notes: Requires driving all the way past Glenwood Springs to the Grand Mesa.
logistics.lodging_notes: Grand Junction or Palisade are the closest major towns, about 45 mins away.

metadata.reputation: Unique, flat-topped mountain riding on the edge of the desert.
metadata.terrain_character: Incredible glade skiing and boulder drops. Very flat on top, but steeper at the bottom.
metadata.snow_quality_notes: Catches unique western flow storms that miss the rest of Colorado.
metadata.crowd_factor: Extremely low. Very isolated from the I-70 corridor crowds.
metadata.logistics_notes: Great option if a massive storm hits the far western edge of the state.
